data.title: Energy Fuels (TSX:EFR) Is Up 8.4% After First High-Purity Terbium Oxide Output Milestone – What's Changed
data.description:
• In March 2026, Energy Fuels Inc. announced plans to expand heavy rare earth production at its White Mesa Mill and reported initial commercial-scale capabilities for dysprosium, terbium and other elements, alongside existing NdPr output, subject to regulatory approvals and monazite feedstock availability.
  • The company also revealed it has produced its first kilogram of 99.9% pure terbium oxide in Utah, positioning itself as a rare U.S. source of high-purity heavy rare earths for electric vehicle, robotics and defense magnet manufacturers amid ongoing Chinese export controls.
  • We’ll now examine how Energy Fuels’ new high-purity terbium oxide production could reshape its investment narrative around rare earth expansion.

Energy Fuels Investment Narrative Recap

To own Energy Fuels, you need to believe its uranium and rare earth mix can eventually turn today’s losses into sustainable, higher margin production, while securing enough feedstock and funding to build out White Mesa and the mineral sands pipeline. The new heavy rare earth expansion plan and terbium milestone strengthen the rare earth story, but the most immediate swing factor still looks to be execution and costs at Pinyon Plain and White Mesa, with financing needs a key ongoing risk.

Among recent announcements, the March 2026 plan to lift White Mesa’s rare earth capacity, including potential NdPr output above 6,000 tonnes per year and meaningful Dy and Tb volumes, stands out as most connected to the terbium oxide news. It ties the pilot scale success in Dy and Tb directly to a future commercial pathway, while keeping the spotlight on two unresolved catalysts: securing sufficient monazite and moving Phase 2 from concept to a fully funded, permitted project.

data.title: When Will Largo SA (EPA:ALLGO) Become Profitable?
data.description:
With the business potentially at an important milestone, we thought we'd take a closer look at Largo SA's (EPA:ALLGO) future prospects. Largo SA engages in the sale of refurbished digital equipment primarily smartphones, tablets, and laptops in France. The €6.8m market-cap company announced a latest loss of €3.3m on 31 December 2025 for its most recent financial year result. Many investors are wondering about the rate at which Largo will turn a profit, with the big question being “when will the company breakeven?” We've put together a brief outline of industry analyst expectations for the company, its year of breakeven and its implied growth rate.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

According to some industry analysts covering Largo, breakeven is near. They expect the company to post a final loss in 2026, before turning a profit of €100k in 2027. So, the company is predicted to breakeven just over a year from now. In order to meet this breakeven date, we calculated the rate at which the company must grow year-on-year. It turns out an average annual growth rate of 105% is expected, which is extremely buoyant. If this rate turns out to be too aggressive, the company may become profitable much later than analysts predict.

Given this is a high-level overview, we won’t go into details of Largo's upcoming projects, but, take into account that generally a high growth rate is not out of the ordinary, particularly when a company is in a period of investment.

View our latest analysis for Largo

One thing we would like to bring into light with Largo is it currently has negative equity on its balance sheet. This can sometimes arise from accounting methods used to deal with accumulated losses from prior years, which are viewed as liabilities carried forward until it cancels out in the future. Oftentimes, losses exist only on paper but other times, it can be a red flag.

data.title: Oxford Instruments Insider Ups Holding By 134% During Year
data.description:
From what we can see, insiders were net buyers in Oxford Instruments plc's (LON:OXIG ) during the past 12 months. That is, insiders acquired the stock in greater numbers than they sold it.

While insider transactions are not the most important thing when it comes to long-term investing, logic dictates you should pay some attention to whether insiders are buying or selling shares.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

The Last 12 Months Of Insider Transactions At Oxford Instruments

In the last twelve months, the biggest single purchase by an insider was when CEO & Director Richard Tyson bought UK£151k worth of shares at a price of UK£19.22 per share. Even though the purchase was made at a significantly lower price than the recent price (UK£25.00), we still think insider buying is a positive. Because it occurred at a lower valuation, it doesn't tell us much about whether insiders might find today's price attractive.

Richard Tyson purchased 15.38k shares over the year. The average price per share was UK£19.23. The chart below shows insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last year. If you click on the chart, you can see all the individual transactions, including the share price, individual, and the date!

See our latest analysis for Oxford Instruments

There are always plenty of stocks that insiders are buying. If investing in lesser known companies is your style, you could take a look at this free list of companies. (Hint: insiders have been buying them).

Does Oxford Instruments Boast High Insider Ownership?

I like to look at how many shares insiders own in a company, to help inform my view of how aligned they are with insiders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. It appears that Oxford Instruments insiders own 1.5% of the company, worth about UK£21m. While this is a strong but not outstanding level of insider ownership, it's enough to indicate some alignment between management and smaller shareholders.

So What Does This Data Suggest About Oxford Instruments Insiders?

There haven't been any insider transactions in the last three months -- that doesn't mean much. On a brighter note, the transactions over the last year are encouraging. Insiders do have a stake in Oxford Instruments and their transactions don't cause us concern. In addition to knowing about insider transactions going on, it's beneficial to identify the risks facing Oxford Instruments. While conducting our analysis, we found that Oxford Instruments has 2 warning signs and it would be unwise to ignore them.

If you would prefer to check out another company -- one with potentially superior financials -- then do not miss this free list of interesting companies, that have HIGH return on equity and low debt.

For the purposes of this article, insiders are those individuals who report their transactions to the relevant regulatory body. We currently account for open market transactions and private dispositions of direct interests only, but not derivative transactions or indirect interests.
